event: Early hunter-gatherer peoples, often called the Guanahatabey, settle the island of Cuba, living as cave-dwellers and foragers.
Ciboney (Siboney) peoples spread across Cuba, subsisting on fishing, shellfish gathering, and small-scale foraging.
Taino (Arawak) agriculturalists migrate from Hispaniola into eastern Cuba, introducing cassava cultivation, pottery, and village life.
Taino chiefdoms (cacicazgos) become well established across much of Cuba, organizing settlements, agriculture, and trade.
Christopher Columbus lands on the northeastern coast of Cuba during his first voyage and claims the island for Spain.
Diego Velazquez de Cuellar begins the Spanish conquest of Cuba, founding Baracoa as the first European settlement.
The Taino leader Hatuey is captured and burned at the stake by the Spanish, becoming a lasting symbol of indigenous resistance.
Spanish settlers found Santiago de Cuba and other early towns, organizing the island under the encomienda labor system.
Havana is established at its permanent harbor site and grows into a key port for Spanish fleets crossing the Atlantic.
Havana is designated the capital of Spanish Cuba, becoming a strategic hub for treasure fleets and colonial trade.
British forces capture Havana during the Seven Years' War, briefly opening Cuba to expanded trade and slave imports.
Britain returns Havana to Spain under the Treaty of Paris in exchange for Florida.
The Haitian Revolution prompts a surge in sugar and coffee production in Cuba, accelerating the expansion of plantation slavery.
Spain formally agrees to abolish the transatlantic slave trade to Cuba, though illegal slave imports continue for decades.
Carlos Manuel de Cespedes issues the Grito de Yara and frees his slaves, launching the Ten Years' War for independence.
The Pact of Zanjon ends the Ten Years' War without securing independence, though it promises some reforms.
Slavery is fully abolished in Cuba, ending centuries of enslaved labor on the island's plantations.
Jose Marti and Maximo Gomez relaunch the war of independence; Marti is killed in battle at Dos Rios.
The explosion of the USS Maine in Havana harbor triggers the Spanish-American War, ending in Spanish defeat and withdrawal.
The United States establishes a military occupation government over Cuba following the departure of Spain.
Cuba becomes an independent republic, though the Platt Amendment grants the United States broad rights to intervene.
Cuba leases Guantanamo Bay to the United States for a naval base under terms tied to the Platt Amendment.
Gerardo Machado becomes president and soon rules as a dictator, provoking widespread political unrest.
A revolution overthrows Machado, and Fulgencio Batista emerges as a dominant power behind a series of governments.
The Platt Amendment is abrogated, ending the formal U.S. right to intervene in Cuban affairs while keeping the Guantanamo base.
Cuba adopts a progressive new constitution, and Fulgencio Batista is elected president.
Cuba becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Fulgencio Batista seizes power in a military coup, canceling elections and establishing an authoritarian regime.
Fidel Castro leads a failed attack on the Moncada Barracks in Santiago, marking the start of his revolutionary movement.
Castro, Che Guevara, and other rebels land aboard the yacht Granma and begin a guerrilla campaign in the Sierra Maestra.
Batista flees as Castro's forces triumph, bringing the Cuban Revolution to power on January 1.
Cuba nationalizes U.S.-owned businesses and signs trade deals with the Soviet Union, prompting a U.S. economic embargo.
The U.S.-backed Bay of Pigs invasion is defeated, and Castro declares the revolution to be socialist.
The Cuban Missile Crisis brings the world to the brink of nuclear war before Soviet missiles are withdrawn from the island.
The Communist Party of Cuba is formally established as the sole legal party under Fidel Castro's leadership.
A new socialist constitution is adopted, formalizing the structures of the Cuban state with Fidel Castro as president.
The Mariel boatlift sends roughly 125,000 Cubans to the United States over several months.
The collapse of the Soviet Union ends crucial subsidies, plunging Cuba into the economic crisis known as the Special Period.
The U.S. tightens its embargo through the Helms-Burton Act after Cuban forces shoot down two exile aircraft.
Pope John Paul II makes a historic visit to Cuba, calling for greater openness on the island.
An ailing Fidel Castro temporarily transfers power to his brother Raul Castro.
Raul Castro formally becomes president and begins introducing limited economic reforms.
Cuba and the United States restore diplomatic relations and reopen embassies after more than five decades of hostility.
Fidel Castro dies at age 90, marking the end of an era for the Cuban Revolution.
Miguel Diaz-Canel becomes president, the first Cuban leader from outside the Castro family since 1959.
Cuba adopts a new constitution that recognizes private property and market elements while reaffirming socialism.
Widespread anti-government protests erupt across Cuba amid economic hardship, shortages, and the pandemic.
